key_departures.impact: Released with post-June 1 designation, generating an NFL-record ~$99.2M dead cap hit split over 2026 ($55.2M) and 2027 ($43.8M). Was benched in favor of seventh-round rookie Quinn Ewers late in 2025. Departure was necessary but leaves a massive cap anchor. Replacement (Malik Willis) immediately signed.

Released Feb. 16 ahead of $16M contract guarantee trigger. Suffered dislocated knee and torn ACL in Week 4 of 2025 season; recovery timeline unclear. Was the WR1 and the offensive centerpiece for four seasons. Departure leaves a massive hole at WR1 with no comparable replacement signed.

Released with post-June 1 designation. Led team with 8.5 sacks in 2025. Represented a $31M cap hit. Signed 3yr/$43.5M with a division rival, making his departure doubly painful. Chop Robinson is now the only experienced pass rusher on the roster.

Traded to Jets for a 2026 seventh-round pick — a cap-clearing move. Three-time first-team All-Pro and five-time Pro Bowler. Started 14 games in 2025. Extremely low return for an elite player; move was purely financial. Creates a significant void at starting safety.
Released after suffering a pectoral injury in Week 1 of 2025. Signed 3yr/$24M the prior year but played in only one game. Cap casualty. Creates a need at starting IOG.

Former undrafted nickel starter missed all of 2025 with a knee injury. Signed 1-year prove-it deal with KC. Had 180 career tackles and 28 PBUs in 3 seasons with Miami. Departure further depletes an already thin cornerback room.
Non-tendered RFA. Blocking specialist who played 50%+ offensive snaps for the first time in 2025. Departure leaves the Dolphins thin behind Dulcich, though Ben Sims was added as his replacement.
Depth/special teams linebacker. Low impact departure.
Released. Only 11 catches for 89 yards in 2025. Minimal impact departure.
Departed via free agency to LAC. Was not a starter but adds to interior OL attrition.
Released after 8 seasons. Replaced immediately by Zane Gonzalez.

signings.analysis: Willis gets the first two years fully guaranteed ($1.25M base in 2026, $21.5M in 2027) with a team out after Year 2. At $22.5M AAV he is a mid-tier starter deal — not a full market commitment to a franchise QB. He went 2-1 as a starter for Green Bay with 78.7% completion rate and zero INTs in 11 appearances, but that is an extremely small sample. The $45M in two-year guarantees signals Miami views him as the starter for at least 2026-27, but the team-friendly Year 3 escape clause indicates this is bridge/audition structure. Quinn Ewers remains on the roster as competition. QB is partially addressed for 2026-27; Miami will likely still want a QB in the 2026 or 2027 draft.

One-year minimum-value deal. Uche had 11.5 sacks with the Patriots in 2022 but has only 10 sacks combined across five other seasons. In 2025 he had 1 sack in 244 snaps for Philadelphia and lost his defensive role mid-season. Adds rotational depth alongside Chop Robinson, Cameron Goode, and Robert Beal Jr., but is not a starter-caliber solution. EDGE remains a critical draft/free agency need. Miami still needs a major overhaul at the position.

Former 49ers fifth-round pick (2023) not tendered as an RFA. Has 36 career tackles and 1 sack in 25 games, splitting time between defense and special teams. Purely a depth/special teams add. Does nothing to address the starter need at EDGE.

Re-signed after finishing 2025 as Miami's third-leading receiver in just 10 games (26 rec, 336 yds). Led all TEs in yards after catch (7.6) and yards per target (10.2). One-year deal at $3.25M with $2.91M guaranteed. Below the starter-tier AAV but he's clearly the TE1 in Miami's plans. The one-year commitment signals the team is not confident he's their long-term answer; a TE could still be targeted in mid-rounds of the draft. Partially addresses the TE need.

Blocking TE with ties to new GM Jon-Eric Sullivan (from their time together in Green Bay). Replaces Julian Hill as the in-line blocking option. One-year minimum deal. Does not change the TE need picture beyond providing a body.

One-year deal for the speedy local product. Atwell had 6 catches for 192 yards in only 10 games in 2025 after hamstring issues and scheme changes in LA. Was paid $10M/year by the Rams in 2025 — his 2026 Miami deal is a deep discount prove-it contract. He has deep threat ability but has never been a consistent WR1. Does nothing to address the loss of Tyreek Hill. WR remains a critical need; Jaylen Waddle is the clear WR1 and the depth behind him (Atwell, Malik Washington, Terrace Marshall Jr.) is very thin.

Re-signed off the Rams' practice squad mid-2025. Has 39 career games with limited starting experience. Has great size (6-2, 198) but the Dolphins themselves acknowledge he is not a starting-caliber CB. One-year minimum deal. CB room remains extremely barren and is one of the team's most critical draft needs.

Most experienced CB added in free agency — started 6 games for Colts in 2023 and 9-10 for Titans in 2024-25. Undrafted out of Arizona Cardinals in 2022. One-year minimum deal. Provides the most credible competition for a CB2/CB3 role but is not a clear starting solution. CB need unchanged at its core.

Former seventh-round pick (2023, Bills) who bounced to Texans, Patriots. 29 career games, 6 starts. Primarily a special teams contributor. One-year deal. Adds no meaningful change to the CB1/CB2 need.

2021 fourth-round pick (Cardinals) with 64 career games and 37 starts. Has 179 tackles, 20 PBUs, 3 INTs. Miami's fourth CB signing in as many days, all at veteran minimum. Adds competition depth. Is the fourth South Floridian signed (local connections being a theme). CB need unchanged at a critical tier.

Spent 2025 on IR early then was activated Week 10. Has played both safety and cornerback. Primarily a special teams contributor. One-year deal. Does nothing to address the void left by trading Minkah Fitzpatrick. S remains a significant need.

Former Packers safety who follows new coach Jeff Hafley from Green Bay. Was not tendered as an RFA. Appeared in 16 of 17 games before an ankle injury. Primarily a special teams contributor. Minimum deal. Does not solve the starting safety need.

Re-signed on one-year deal. Played 131 defensive snaps in 2025 — small sample. Training camp standout but hasn't carried it over consistently. Provides depth, special teams value, and Super Bowl pedigree. LB was not a critical pre-FA need and this doesn't change that.
Re-signed on one-year deal. Had 12 tackles in 12 games in 2025. Former RFA not tendered, now brought back cheaply. Rotational DL depth. Not a starter; IDL remains a moderate need.

One-year deal. Replaces Jason Sanders who was released. Gonzalez has been reliable inside 40 yards and is 22-of-23 on FG attempts between 40-49 yards over the past three seasons. K was a need created by Sanders' release and is now filled.
Re-signed as kicker depth/competition alongside Gonzalez. Was 27-of-29 on FGs in 2025. Now competes with Gonzalez for the starting role. Redundant with Gonzalez signing but low cost.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Willis signing (3yr/$67.5M, $45M guaranteed) makes QB a bridge-addressed need for 2026-27. He is the designated starter with two years of full guarantees. However, the team-friendly Year 3 exit, the small sample size, and Quinn Ewers' presence all signal Miami may still want a QB with a later draft pick in 2026 or as their top priority in 2027. Downgrade from critical to moderate — monitor but not a Day 1 pick necessity in 2026.

Chubb released (went to Bills), Jaelan Phillips traded mid-2025. Only Chop Robinson has meaningful production on the roster. Uche (1-yr minimum) and Beal (1-yr minimum) are rotational bodies, not starters. EDGE is arguably the single biggest draft need on the roster. New coach Jeff Hafley will struggle to generate a quality pass rush. Should be a top-3 draft priority at picks 11 and/or Day 2.

Hill released (torn ACL, age 32). Atwell signed to a minimum prove-it deal — not a WR1 solution. Jaylen Waddle is the only reliable receiver. The cupboard is bare. WR is now a critical need alongside EDGE. Miami needs a legitimate WR2 at minimum; the Waddle-plus-Atwell combo is severely inadequate for the passing game they need to develop Willis.

Kohou (lost to KC), Rasul Douglas (unsigned), Noah Igbinoghene (departed) all gone. The four CB signings (Baker, Austin, Green, Wilson) are all veteran minimum one-year depth players — none have legitimate starting pedigree at starter-caliber AAV. New HC Jeff Hafley has a strong CB development background which offers some optimism, but the room is historically thin. CB must be addressed in early-to-mid rounds of the 2026 draft.

Minkah Fitzpatrick traded to Jets for a seventh-round pick — an elite safety sent to a division rival for essentially nothing. Johnson and Anderson are special-teams bodies, not starters. Miami has no legitimate starting safety under contract. S joins EDGE, WR, and CB as a critical draft need. Should be addressed no later than Day 2.

James Daniels released (injury), Cole Strange left to Chargers, Daniel Brunskill unsigned. Aaron Brewer (C) and Austin Jackson (OT, restructured to $7M/1yr) remain under contract but the interior OL depth is thin. No FA signings at IOL. Need unchanged at moderate-to-high — a guard or center should be targeted in the mid-rounds of the draft.

Austin Jackson restructured to stay ($7M, 1yr, $5.475M gtd). No other significant OT moves. The line remains a concern but with Jackson retained it is not a Day 1 priority. Could still look for a developmental tackle in mid-rounds.
Matthew Butler re-signed on a minimum deal. Rotational depth only. No starter-level IDL addition. Need is moderate; draft capital or a cheaper FA later in the process likely needed.

Dulcich re-signed (1yr/$3.25M) and Sims added as the blocker. Dulcich is legitimate TE1 candidate based on 2025 performance but is on a one-year prove-it deal. Julian Hill lost to Patriots. TE need is partially addressed for 2026 but a developmental TE in the mid-rounds makes sense.

key_additions.headline: New QB1 on a team-friendly deal with $45M in two-year guarantees, replacing Tua Tagovailoa in a full franchise reset
Re-signed after breakout stretch in 2025 (26 rec, 336 yds in 10 games); leads all TEs in YAC and yards per target
Depth pass rusher with 11.5-sack upside, added on a minimum deal to rebuild a depleted EDGE room
Most experienced CB addition with 15+ starts in the last two seasons; competes for starting role in a barren cornerback room
Miami native returns home as a deep-threat flier; one-year discount deal following $10M Rams contract
New starting kicker replaces released Jason Sanders; 22-of-23 on FG attempts from 40-49 yards over past three seasons

key_losses.headline: NFL-record dead cap hit ends the Tagovailoa era after 6 seasons and one playoff appearance; benched late in 2025 for poor play
All-time great WR released after torn ACL and dislocated knee in Week 4 of 2025; leaves Miami with no true WR1
Team's top pass rusher (8.5 sacks in 2025) cut for cap reasons and immediately signed by a division rival

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded for 2026 7th-round pick; signed 3yr/$40M w/ Jets
key_losses.headline: Three-time All-Pro safety traded intra-division for minimal return in pure cap-clearing move
Fan-favorite undrafted slot CB departs after missing 2025 season with knee injury; further depletes an already thin CB room
Biggest 2025 OL signing played just 3 snaps before pec injury ended his season; cap casualty heading into 2026

notes: Contract monetary values for minimum-deal signings (Uche, Beal, Green, Baker, Austin, Wilson, Johnson, Anderson, Gay, Butler, Sims) are estimates based on reported one-year deals at or near veteran minimum; exact figures not publicly disclosed per multiple sources. Willis, Dulcich, and Gonzalez contract figures sourced from NFL Network (Rapoport/Pelissero), ESPN (Fowler), and Spotrac. Miami is operating in full rebuild mode under first-year HC Jeff Hafley and first-year GM Jon-Eric Sullivan with significant dead cap from Tagovailoa release (~$55.2M in 2026 alone) limiting spending. Nearly every FA addition is a one-year, low-cost deal. The Dolphins hold the No. 11 overall pick in the 2026 draft and have the seventh-most draft capital in the league — the draft, not free agency, will be the primary roster-building vehicle.
